content.text: Why AI Application Repos Need Stronger Branch Protection

A standard GitHub repository typically protects only the main branch: require a PR, require one reviewer, and require CI to pass. For a Claude application repository, this baseline is insufficient. The repository contains artefacts that govern AI behaviour in production — system prompts, tool schemas, behaviour specs, and eval suites. A prompt change merged without a drift check can cause the deployed prompt to diverge from the registered hash, breaking the audit trail. A tool schema change merged without a backward-compatibility check can cause tool calls to fail for all callers that sent the old field names.

Branch protection rules for AI application repos extend the standard protection to enforce the change control process mechanically. If the CI job that checks prompt drift is not a required status check, engineers can merge without running it. Required status checks make skipping impossible.

This sub-subtopic defines the three layers of branch protection for a Claude application repository, the specific status checks that must be required at each layer, and how to configure them in GitHub.

attrs.alt: Three protection layers: main (strongest, 2 approvals, eval suite + drift check required, no bypass); release/* (strong, 1 approval, full eval suite required, release-manager bypass only); prompt/ tool/ agent/ branches (type-specific, eval suite + lifetime check + drift check for prompt branches + schema compat for tool branches).

content.text: Three layers of branch protection map to three levels of risk. The main branch carries the highest risk because a merge directly affects what all future release branches will contain. Release branches carry medium risk because a change to a deployed release affects live users. Feature branches carry lower risk, but type-specific checks enforce the correct review process for each change type.
The main branch must be the hardest to merge into. Every merge to main is a deployment candidate; every unreviewed or untested merge is a potential production incident.
No direct pushes to main; every change goes through a PR
Two independent reviewers; catches problems one reviewer might miss
A new commit invalidates previous approvals; reviewers must re-approve
CI must be green before merge is possible
Require branches to be up to date
Branch must be rebased on main before merge; prevents merge races
No rewriting of main branch history
No exceptions — not even repository admins

# Configure main branch protection via GitHub CLI
# (requires gh CLI 2.x and admin access to the repository)

gh api -X PUT repos/YOUR-ORG/claude-app/branches/main/protection   --field required_status_checks[strict]=true   --field "required_status_checks[contexts][]=lint-and-type"   --field "required_status_checks[contexts][]=unit-tests"   --field "required_status_checks[contexts][]=eval-suite"   --field "required_status_checks[contexts][]=drift-check"   --field enforce_admins=true   --field "required_pull_request_reviews[required_approving_review_count]=2"   --field "required_pull_request_reviews[dismiss_stale_reviews]=true"   --field allow_force_pushes=false   --field allow_deletions=false

Release branches carry live deployments. The protection must prevent force-pushes that would rewrite the deployed version's history, while allowing the release manager to cherry-pick hotfixes with one reviewer (not two, since hotfixes are time-critical).
All changes via PR, even cherry-picks
Hotfixes are time-critical; one reviewer is sufficient for cherry-picks
Full eval suite always runs on release branches
Deployed versions must have immutable history
Release branches cannot be deleted until formally retired
Emergency cherry-picks in the middle of the night
Layer 3: Type-Specific Status Checks for Feature Branches

GitHub branch protection rules can target branch name patterns. Use this to apply different required status checks to different branch types. A prompt/ branch must pass the drift check; a tool/ branch must pass the schema compatibility check; a fix/ branch needs neither (since it changes code, not prompts or schemas).
Required checks beyond lint + unit tests
Prompt changes need hash registration and lifetime enforcement
Tool changes need backward compat verification
Eval additions are code-only; eval suite will include new cases on next PR
Bug fixes run evals to catch regressions
Risk mitigations often touch prompts; drift check required
CODEOWNERS: Enforcing Review by the Right People

GitHub CODEOWNERS allows the repository to require that specific teams or individuals review changes to specific files or directories. For a Claude application, this means that any change to the system prompt, tool schemas, or behaviour spec automatically requests review from the team responsible for those artefacts.

# .github/CODEOWNERS
# Every change to the system prompt requires review from the AI Safety team
prompts/                    @your-org/ai-safety-team

# Tool schema changes require the API team (they own the contract with callers)
tools/                      @your-org/api-team
src/tools/                  @your-org/api-team

# Behaviour spec changes require the product owner and AI Safety team
specs/behaviour_spec.json   @your-org/ai-safety-team @your-org/product-owners

# Eval suite changes require AI Safety team review
specs/eval_suite.json       @your-org/ai-safety-team

# Risk register changes require the risk owner
risk/                       @your-org/risk-owners

# CI and branch protection config changes require DevOps + AI Safety team
.github/                    @your-org/devops @your-org/ai-safety-team
terraform/                  @your-org/devops

# Default: all engineers can review
*                           @your-org/engineering

CODEOWNERS enforcement is strongest when combined with the 'Require review from Code Owners' setting in branch protection. With that setting enabled, a PR that touches prompts/ must receive approval from @ai-safety-team specifically — general engineering approval is not sufficient.

Requiring signed commits on main and release branches means every merge commit has a cryptographic signature that ties it to the committer's verified identity. This is important for a Claude application because the commit history is the audit trail: if a prompt change caused a production incident, the signed commit proves who merged it and when.

# Setting up commit signing with GPG (developer workstation)

# Generate a GPG key if you don't have one
gpg --full-generate-key  # Choose RSA 4096 and your work email

# Get your key ID
gpg --list-secret-keys --keyid-format LONG
# Output example: sec   rsa4096/ABC123DEF456 2025-01-01 [SC]

# Configure git to sign commits automatically
git config --global user.signingkey ABC123DEF456
git config --global commit.gpgsign true

# Export your public key and add it to your GitHub account
gpg --armor --export ABC123DEF456 | pbcopy
# Paste into GitHub → Settings → SSH and GPG keys → New GPG key

# Verify signing works
git commit --allow-empty -m "test signed commit"
git log --show-signature -1
# Should show: gpg: Good signature from "Your Name <you@example.com>"

For CI pipelines, signed commits are typically configured using a bot account with a dedicated GPG key stored in GitHub Actions secrets. The key is imported in the CI step before the commit is created.

Branch protection rules can be viewed in the GitHub repository settings, but a Claude application repository should also have an automated compliance check that verifies the protection rules are configured correctly. This prevents drift where an admin accidentally disables a required check.

# .github/workflows/branch-protection-audit.yml
name: Branch Protection Audit

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 9 * * MON"  # Every Monday at 09:00 UTC
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  audit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - name: Audit branch protection rules
        run: python3 scripts/audit_branch_protection.py
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          GITHUB_REPOSITORY: ${{ github.repository }}
Required checks not applied to branch pattern
prompt/ branch merges without running drift-check
Verify pattern 'prompt/*' has drift-check as a required status check
Repository admins can merge without CI passing; used during 'emergencies'
Set enforce_admins=true and handle true emergencies via the kill switch, not by bypassing CI
Prompt changes approved by an engineer who is not on the AI Safety team
Reviewer approves; author pushes a new commit; old approval still counts
Enable dismiss_stale_reviews so new commits require fresh approval
Branch protection applied only to main, not release/*
Hotfix cherry-picked to release branch without running the full eval suite
Add a branch protection rule for the release/* pattern

Branch protection rules for Claude application repositories operate at three layers. The main branch requires two approvals, requires lint-and-type, unit-tests, eval-suite, and drift-check status checks, enforces admins (no bypass), blocks force-pushes, and requires signed commits. Release branches require one approval, require eval-suite-full and release-integrity checks, block force-pushes, and allow bypass only for the release-manager team in emergencies. Feature branches use type-specific required checks: prompt/ branches add drift-check and branch-lifetime-check; tool/ branches add schema-compat; agent/ branches add all four. CODEOWNERS enforces that prompt, tool schema, and behaviour spec changes are reviewed by the teams accountable for those artefacts. An automated weekly audit job verifies that protection rules have not been accidentally disabled.
